f8bea835f63ad19f.summary: Electric current (I = Q/t) is flow of charge (electrons). Unit: Ampere (A). Circuit: cell/battery, wires, switch, load (bulb, resistor). Open circuit (no current) vs closed circuit (current flows). Direction: conventional (positive to negative) vs electron flow (negative to positive).

chunks.title: Understanding Electric Current and Circuit
chunks.content:
**Electric Current and Circuit** is a key topic in Electricity for CBSE Class X Science.

**Electric current (I):** Rate of flow of electric charge.

I = Q / t

Where Q = charge (Coulombs), t = time (seconds)

1 Ampere = 1 Coulomb / 1 second

**Charge of electron:** 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
**Direction:** Conventional current flows from positive to negative terminal (opposite to electron flow).

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Electric Circuit Components
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Cell/Battery:** Provides potential difference (voltage). Converts chemical energy → electrical energy. 1 cell = ~1.5 V
• **Switch:** Controls current flow (open = no current, closed = current flows)
• **Load (bulb, resistor, motor):** Device that consumes electrical energy
• **Wires:** Connect components (Cu or Al, good conductors, insulated)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Types of Circuits
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Open circuit:** Gap in path → current cannot flow. Bulb doesn't light. Switch OFF
• **Closed circuit:** Complete path → current flows. Bulb lights. Switch ON
• **Short circuit:** Very low resistance path (wire directly across terminals). Very high current → overheating, sparks, fire risk

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A current of 2 A flows through a bulb for 5 minutes. Calculate charge that flows.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Given: I = 2 A, t = 5 min = 5×60 = 300 s
**Step 2:** I = Q/t → Q = I×t
**Step 3:** Q = 2 × 300 = 600 C

**Answer:** Charge = 600 C.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What is the SI unit of current?

**Answer:** Ampere (A). 1 A = 1 C/s.

---

**Q2.** Why does a bulb not glow when the switch is open?

**Answer:** Open circuit = gap → no current flow → no energy to bulb.

---

**Q3.** Calculate number of electrons flowing per second through a wire carrying 1 A. (e=1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C)

**Answer:** n = Q/e = 1/(1.6×10⁻¹⁹) = 6.25×10¹⁸ electrons/s.

db56b7b2e86d3c9d.summary: Potential difference (V = W/Q) is work done per unit charge. Unit: Volt (V). 1 V = 1 J/C. Cell provides potential difference. Voltmeter measures V (connected in parallel). Ammeter measures I (connected in series).

chunks.title: Understanding Electric Potential and Potential Difference
chunks.content:
**Electric Potential and Potential Difference** is a key topic in Electricity for CBSE Class X Science.

**Electric potential (V):** Work done to bring a unit positive charge from infinity to a point in electric field.

**Potential difference (ΔV):** Work done to move a unit charge from one point to another.

V = W / Q

Where W = work done (Joules), Q = charge (Coulombs)

1 Volt = 1 Joule / 1 Coulomb

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Measuring Instruments
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Voltmeter:** Measures potential difference between TWO points. Connected in PARALLEL across the component. High resistance (draws negligible current). Symbol: V in a circle
• **Ammeter:** Measures current flowing through a component. Connected in SERIES with the component. Very low resistance (negligible voltage drop). Symbol: A in a circle
• **Galvanometer:** Detects small currents. Can be converted to ammeter or voltmeter

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Cell and Battery
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Cell:** Two electrodes in electrolyte. Chemical reaction creates potential difference (~1.5 V for dry cell). Electrons accumulate at negative terminal
• **Battery:** Two or more cells connected. Series: voltages add (1.5+1.5=3 V). Parallel: same voltage, more current capacity
• **EMF vs Terminal Voltage:** EMF = potential difference when no current flows. Terminal V = voltage when current flows (less due to internal resistance)

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** If 10 J of work is done to move 2 C of charge between two points, what is the potential difference?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Given: W = 10 J, Q = 2 C
**Step 2:** V = W/Q = 10/2 = 5 V

**Answer:** Potential difference = 5 V.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What is the unit of potential difference?

**Answer:** Volt (V). 1 V = 1 J/C.

---

**Q2.** How is a voltmeter connected in a circuit?

**Answer:** In PARALLEL across the component being measured.

---

**Q3.** Why does a cell have a potential difference?

**Answer:** Chemical reaction separates charges → creates potential difference between terminals.

ce8bffdcb0417f97.summary: Ohm's Law: V ∝ I (at constant temperature). V = IR, where R = resistance (Ohm, Ω). I-V graph is a straight line through origin for ohmic conductors. Non-ohmic: diodes, thermistors, bulbs (non-linear I-V).

chunks.title: Understanding Ohm's Law
chunks.content:
**Ohm's Law** is a key topic in Electricity for CBSE Class X Science.

**Ohm's Law (Georg Simon Ohm, 1826):**

The potential difference across a conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it, provided temperature remains constant.

V ∝ I → V/I = constant = R

**V = I × R**

Where R = resistance (unit: Ohm, Ω)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Ohmic and Non-ohmic Conductors
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Ohmic conductors:** Obey Ohm's law. I-V graph is a straight line through origin. Examples: metals (copper, aluminium, iron), electrolytes at constant temperature
• **Non-ohmic conductors:** Do NOT obey Ohm's law. I-V graph is non-linear. Examples: semiconductor diodes, transistors, filament bulb (resistance changes with temperature)
• **Filament bulb:** As current increases, filament heats → resistance increases → slope of I-V curve decreases (not linear)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Resistance and Conductance
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Resistance (R):** Opposition to flow of current. Depends on material, length, cross-section, temperature. R = V/I. Unit: Ohm (Ω)
• **Conductance (G):** Ease of current flow. G = 1/R. Unit: Siemens (S) or mho (℧)
• **Effect of temperature:** For metals, R increases with temperature. For semiconductors, R decreases with temperature (negative temperature coefficient)

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A 12 V battery is connected to a resistor, and 3 A current flows. What is the resistance?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Given: V = 12 V, I = 3 A
**Step 2:** R = V/I = 12/3 = 4 Ω

**Answer:** R = 4 Ω.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** State Ohm's law.

**Answer:** V ∝ I at constant temperature. V/I = R (constant).

---

**Q2.** Why does the resistance of a bulb increase when it glows?

**Answer:** Heating → atoms vibrate more → more collisions for electrons → higher resistance.

---

**Q3.** A wire has 2 A current at 6 V potential difference. What is resistance?

**Answer:** R = 6/2 = 3 Ω.

69d4798f42772378.summary: Resistance depends on: material (ρ), length (L), cross-section area (A), temperature. R = ρL/A. Resistivity ρ is material property (Ω·m). Good conductors have low ρ (Cu 1.7×10⁻⁸), insulators have high ρ (rubber 10¹³). Series: R_eq = R₁+R₂+... Parallel: 1/R_eq = 1/R₁+1/R₂+...

chunks.title: Understanding Factors on which the Resistance of a Conductor depends
chunks.content:
**Factors on which the Resistance of a Conductor depends** is a key topic in Electricity for CBSE Class X Science.

**Resistance depends on four factors:**
1. **Material (resistivity ρ):** Different materials offer different resistance
2. **Length (L):** Longer wire → more resistance (R ∝ L)
3. **Cross-sectional area (A):** Thicker wire → less resistance (R ∝ 1/A)
4. **Temperature:** R increases with temperature for metals

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Resistivity
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Formula:** R = ρ × L / A, where ρ = resistivity (Ω·m)
• **Resistivity:** Material property independent of size/shape. Conductors: low ρ (Cu=1.72×10⁻⁸, Al=2.65×10⁻⁸, Fe=9.8×10⁻⁸ Ω·m). Insulators: high ρ (rubber≈10¹³, glass≈10¹⁰ Ω·m). Alloys: higher than pure metals (nichrome=10⁻⁶ Ω·m, used in heating elements)
• **Temperature coefficient:** α. R_T = R₀(1 + αΔT). For metals α > 0 (R increases). For semiconductors α < 0 (R decreases with temperature)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Applications
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Heating elements (nichrome):** High resistivity, high melting point, does not oxidise at high T
• **Fuse wire:** Low melting point (tin-lead alloy). High current melts it → breaks circuit (safety)
• **Transmission cables (Cu, Al):** Low resistivity → less power loss. Thick wires for long distance

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A wire of length 2 m and area 0.5 mm² has resistance 6.8 Ω. Find its resistivity.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Given: L=2 m, A=0.5 mm²=0.5×10⁻⁶ m², R=6.8 Ω
**Step 2:** ρ = R×A/L = 6.8×0.5×10⁻⁶/2 = 1.7×10⁻⁶ Ω·m

**Answer:** ρ = 1.7×10⁻⁶ Ω·m (nichrome).

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** How does resistance change if wire length is doubled?

**Answer:** R ∝ L. If length doubled, resistance doubled.

---

**Q2.** Why are heating elements made of nichrome?

**Answer:** High resistivity, high MP, does not oxidise at high temperatures.

---

**Q3.** Wire of length L, area A has resistance R. If stretched to twice length (same volume), find new R.

**Answer:** New A = A/2 (volume constant). R' = ρ×2L/(A/2) = 4ρL/A = 4R.

f68751f396cdfa9c.summary: Series: R_eq = R₁+R₂+R₃, same current, voltage divides. Parallel: 1/R_eq = 1/R₁+1/R₂, same voltage, current divides. Advantages: parallel gives independent operation, lower equivalent resistance. Household wiring is parallel.

chunks.title: Understanding Resistors in Series and Parallel
chunks.content:
**Resistors in Series and Parallel** is a key topic in Electricity for CBSE Class X Science.

**Series combination:** Resistors connected end-to-end. Same current flows through each.

**Parallel combination:** Resistors connected across same two points. Same voltage across each.

**Equivalent resistance (R_eq):** Single resistor that replaces the combination.

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Series Combination
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Formula:** R_eq = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ + ... + Rₙ (for n resistors in series)
• **Current:** Same in all resistors (I = I₁ = I₂ = I₃)
• **Voltage:** Divides among resistors (V = V₁+V₂+V₃). V₁ = IR₁, V₂ = IR₂, etc.
• **Advantage:** Simple, voltage divider circuit
• **Disadvantage:** If one bulb fails, entire circuit breaks (e.g., old Christmas lights)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Parallel Combination
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Formula:** 1/R_eq = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃ + ... + 1/Rₙ. R_eq = (R₁×R₂)/(R₁+R₂) for 2 resistors
• **Voltage:** Same across all resistors (V = V₁ = V₂ = V₃)
• **Current:** Divides among branches (I = I₁+I₂+I₃). I₁ = V/R₁, I₂ = V/R₂, etc.
• **Advantages:** Independent operation (one bulb fails, others work). Each device gets full voltage. Lower total resistance → more current from battery

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Three resistors 4Ω, 6Ω, and 12Ω are connected in parallel. Find R_eq.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** 1/R_eq = 1/4 + 1/6 + 1/12 = (3+2+1)/12 = 6/12 = 1/2
**Step 2:** R_eq = 2 Ω

**Answer:** R_eq = 2 Ω (less than each individual resistance).

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** Two resistors 6Ω and 3Ω in series. Find R_eq.

**Answer:** R_eq = 6+3 = 9 Ω.

---

**Q2.** Two resistors 6Ω and 3Ω in parallel. Find R_eq.

**Answer:** 1/R_eq = 1/6+1/3 = 1/2 → R_eq = 2 Ω.

---

**Q3.** Why is household wiring done in parallel?

**Answer:** Each appliance gets same voltage (220V), independent ON/OFF, total current = sum of individual.

802125cb8704627f.summary: Joule's law of heating: H = I²Rt. Current through resistor → heat. Applications: electric heater, iron, bulb (heating of filament). Filament bulbs: ~90% energy as heat, ~10% as light. Tungsten filament (high MP, 3422°C). Fuse: safety device melts at high current.

chunks.title: Understanding Heating Effect of Electric Current
chunks.content:
**Heating Effect of Electric Current** is a key topic in Electricity for CBSE Class X Science.

**Joule's law of heating:**
Heat produced when current flows through a resistor:

H = I² × R × t

Where I = current (A), R = resistance (Ω), t = time (s)

**Alternative forms:**
H = VIt (since V=IR)
H = V²t/R

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Applications of Heating Effect
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Electric heater/iron:** Nichrome coil (high resistivity, high MP) converts electrical energy to heat. I²R loss is USEFUL here
• **Incandescent bulb:** Tungsten filament (MP=3422°C) heated to white hot (~2500°C) → emits light. ~90% heat, ~10% light (inefficient). Inert gas filling (Ar/N₂) prevents oxidation
• **Electric fuse:** Thin wire (low melting point alloy) in series. If current exceeds safe limit → I²R heat melts fuse → breaks circuit → protects appliances from damage

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Electric Power
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Power (P):** Rate at which electrical energy is consumed or produced
• P = VI = I²R = V²/R (Watt, W)
• **Energy (E):** E = P×t = VIt (Joules or kWh)
• **kWh (unit):** 1 kWh = 3.6×10⁶ J. Electricity bills in kWh (units)

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** An electric heater draws 5 A at 220 V for 2 hours. Calculate (a) power (b) energy in kWh.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** (a) P = VI = 220×5 = 1100 W = 1.1 kW
**Step 2:** (b) E = P×t = 1.1 kW × 2 h = 2.2 kWh

**Answer:** Power = 1100 W. Energy = 2.2 kWh (units).

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** State Joule's law of heating.

**Answer:** H = I²Rt. Heat = current² × resistance × time.

---

**Q2.** Why is tungsten used in bulb filaments?

**Answer:** Highest melting point (3422°C), can glow white hot without melting.

---

**Q3.** An iron of 1000 W runs at 250 V. Calculate current and resistance.

**Answer:** I = 1000/250 = 4 A. R = V/I = 250/4 = 62.5 Ω.

4aadaaddf6b90aee.summary: Electric power P = VI = I²R = V²/R. Unit: Watt (W). Commercial unit: kWh. 1 kWh = 3.6×10⁶ J. Power rating (e.g., 100 W bulb) indicates energy consumption rate. Appliances: power + time → energy consumed.

chunks.title: Understanding Power
chunks.content:
**Power** is a key topic in Electricity for CBSE Class X Science.

**Electric power:** Rate at which electrical work is done or energy is consumed.

**Formulas:**
P = V × I (from V = W/Q and I = Q/t)
P = I² × R (using V=IR)
P = V²/R (using I=V/R)

**SI unit:** Watt (W). 1 W = 1 J/s = 1 V·A

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Commercial Unit and Power Rating
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Kilowatt-hour (kWh):** Energy consumed by a 1 kW appliance in 1 hour. 1 kWh = 1000 W × 3600 s = 3.6×10⁶ J. Electricity bills charge per kWh (per 'unit'). In India: ~₹5-8 per unit
• **Power rating:** Marked on appliances (e.g., 100 W bulb, 2000 W iron, 1500 W AC). Power rating × operating time = energy consumed
• **Bulbs in series:** Total voltage divides. Each bulb gets less voltage → dimmer. 2 bulbs of same rating in series → each gets half voltage → each dissipates 1/4 rated power

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Numerical Problems
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Example:** 100 W bulb operated 10 h/day for 30 days: E = 0.1 kW × 10 h × 30 = 30 kWh = 30 units. Cost at ₹6/unit = ₹180
• **Fuse rating:** I = P/V. 1000 W iron at 220 V: I = 1000/220 ≈ 4.5 A. Use 5 A fuse

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Two bulbs (100 W, 250 V) and (200 W, 250 V) are connected in series across 250 V. Which glows brighter?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** R₁ = V²/P₁ = 250²/100 = 625 Ω. R₂ = 250²/200 = 312.5 Ω
**Step 2:** I = V/(R₁+R₂) = 250/937.5 = 0.267 A
**Step 3:** P₁ (actual) = I²R₁ = (0.267)²×625 = 44.4 W. P₂ = I²R₂ = 22.2 W
**Step 4:** 100 W bulb glows brighter (higher actual power in series)

**Answer:** 100 W bulb glows brighter (44.4 W vs 22.2 W).

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What is 1 kWh in Joules?

**Answer:** 1 kWh = 3.6×10⁶ J.

---

**Q2.** A 1500 W heater runs for 4 h daily. Find energy in kWh for 30 days.

**Answer:** E = 1.5 × 4 × 30 = 180 kWh.

---

**Q3.** Find current drawn by 60 W bulb at 220 V.

**Answer:** I = P/V = 60/220 ≈ 0.27 A.

4533b3f29b0625c6.summary: Magnetism: properties of magnets (N/S poles, attract/repel, earth as magnet). Magnetic field lines: direction N→S outside, closed loops, never cross, crowded near poles (strong field). Compass needle aligns with field lines.

chunks.title: Understanding Magnetic Field and Field Lines
chunks.content:
**Magnetic Field and Field Lines** is a key topic in Magnetic Effects of Electric Current for CBSE Class X Science.

**Magnetism:** A fundamental force. Certain materials (iron, nickel, cobalt) attract other magnetic materials.

**Basic properties:**
• Like poles REPEL, unlike poles ATTRACT
• A freely suspended magnet aligns NS direction (Earth's magnetic field)
• Magnets always have both poles (cannot isolate N or S)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Magnetic Field and Field Lines
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Magnetic field (B):** Region around magnet where magnetic force acts. Vector quantity (has direction). SI unit: Tesla (T)
• **Magnetic field lines (imaginary):** Direction: North → South OUTSIDE magnet. Closed loops (from N→S outside, S→N inside). Never intersect (would give two directions at one point). Crowded = strong field (near poles). Widely spaced = weak field
• **Uniform field:** Parallel, equally spaced lines (between opposite poles of two magnets). **Non-uniform:** Curved, varying spacing

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Earth's Magnetism
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Earth as magnet:** Geomagnetic field. Earth's magnetic south pole is near geographic north pole (and vice versa)
• **Declination:** Angle between geographic meridian and magnetic meridian. Varies with location
• **Inclination (dip):** Angle of magnetic field with horizontal. Equator: 0° (horizontal). Poles: 90° (vertical)
• **Compass:** Small magnetised needle, aligns with Earth's magnetic field. Always points (magnetic) north-south

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Why do magnetic field lines not intersect each other?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** If lines intersected, two directions for magnetic field at one point
**Step 2:** The compass needle can point in only ONE direction at a point
**Step 3:** Contradiction → field lines cannot intersect

**Answer:** If they intersected, magnetic field would have two directions at that point (impossible).

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What is the direction of magnetic field lines outside a magnet?

**Answer:** From North pole to South pole.

---

**Q2.** Which pole of Earth's magnet is near the geographic North Pole?

**Answer:** Earth's magnetic SOUTH pole is near geographic North Pole (that's why N pole of compass points there).

---

**Q3.** What does crowding of field lines indicate?

**Answer:** Stronger magnetic field.

e3594ad70e8a6c2f.summary: Oersted's experiment: current-carrying wire deflects compass (magnetic field around current). Right-hand thumb rule: thumb = current direction, curled fingers = magnetic field direction. Patterns: straight wire (concentric circles), circular loop (like a disc magnet), solenoid (like a bar magnet).

chunks.title: Understanding Magnetic Field due to a Current-Carrying Conductor
chunks.content:
**Magnetic Field due to a Current-Carrying Conductor** is a key topic in Magnetic Effects of Electric Current for CBSE Class X Science.

**Hans Christian Oersted (1820):** Discovered that a compass needle is deflected near a current-carrying wire. This proved the connection between electricity and magnetism.

**Key fact:** Every current-carrying conductor produces a magnetic field around it.

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Patterns of Magnetic Fields
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Straight conductor:** Magnetic field lines are concentric circles around the wire. Direction depends on current direction. Right-hand rule: grasp wire with thumb in current direction → curled fingers show field direction. Field strength B ∝ I/r (more current = stronger, more distance = weaker) 🔄
• **Circular loop:** Field similar to a short bar magnet. Polarity determined by direction of current. Clockwise → S pole. Anticlockwise → N pole. B ∝ I/R. Multiple loops = stronger field
• **Solenoid:** Many circular turns of insulated wire. Field inside solenoid: uniform, strong, parallel to axis. Field pattern similar to a BAR MAGNET. One end = N pole, other = S pole. B ∝ nI (n = number of turns per unit length)

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Electromagnet
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Electromagnet:** Solenoid with soft iron core. Becomes a strong magnet when current flows. Stops being magnetic when current stops
• **Factors affecting strength:** Number of turns, current magnitude, nature of core material (soft iron best). Stronger than permanent magnet, can be controlled
• **Uses:** Electric bells, cranes (lift scrap iron), MRI machines, speakers, relays, magnetic locks

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A current-carrying solenoid is suspended freely. What will be its orientation?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Solenoid behaves like a bar magnet with N and S poles
**Step 2:** Earth's magnetic field will align it like a compass
**Step 3:** The solenoid will align in the N-S direction (like a bar magnet)

**Answer:** Solenoid aligns in N-S direction (like a bar magnet) due to Earth's magnetic field.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What did Oersted's experiment demonstrate?

**Answer:** Current-carrying wire produces a magnetic field (deflects compass).

---

**Q2.** State the right-hand thumb rule.

**Answer:** Grip wire with right hand, thumb = current direction, curled fingers = magnetic field direction.

---

**Q3.** How can the strength of an electromagnet be increased?

**Answer:** Increase number of turns, increase current, use soft iron core.

342bdf01987a78c2.summary: A current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field experiences force (motor effect). Fleming's left-hand rule: thumb = force, index = field, middle = current. F = BIL sin θ. Direction: perpendicular to both B and I. Application: electric motor, loudspeaker.

chunks.title: Understanding Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor in a Magnetic Field
chunks.content:
**Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor in a Magnetic Field** is a key topic in Magnetic Effects of Electric Current for CBSE Class X Science.

**Key discovery:** A current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a FORCE. This is the basis of the electric motor.

**Condition:** The force is maximum when conductor is perpendicular to field; zero when parallel.

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Fleming's Left-Hand Rule
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Rule:** Stretch left hand so thumb, forefinger (index), and middle finger are mutually perpendicular (L-shape)
• **Forefinger:** Direction of Magnetic Field (N→S)
• **Middle finger:** Direction of Current (conventional, +→−)
• **Thumb:** Direction of Force (motion)
• **Formula:** F = BIL sin θ. F = force (N), B = magnetic field (T), I = current (A), L = length (m). θ = angle between B and I

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Applications
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Electric motor:** Current-carrying coil in magnetic field → rotates (converts electrical energy → mechanical energy)
• **Loudspeaker:** Current in voice coil (in magnetic field) → force moves coil → attached cone vibrates → sound
• **Galvanometer:** Current in coil in magnetic field → coil deflects → indicates current. Scale measures deflection

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A conductor of length 0.5 m carrying 3 A is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field of 0.2 T. Find force.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Given: L=0.5 m, I=3 A, B=0.2 T, θ=90°
**Step 2:** F = BIL sin 90° = 0.2 × 3 × 0.5 × 1 = 0.3 N

**Answer:** F = 0.3 N.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** State Fleming's left-hand rule.

**Answer:** Thumb = Force (motion), Forefinger = Field (N→S), Middle finger = Current (+→−).

---

**Q2.** When is the force on a current-carrying conductor maximum in a magnetic field?

**Answer:** When conductor is PERPENDICULAR to magnetic field (θ=90°, sin 90°=1).

---

**Q3.** A conductor parallel to magnetic field carries current. Is there force?

**Answer:** No. θ=0°, sin 0°=0 → F=0.

7a5d4c50c495ea28.summary: Electric motor: converts electrical → mechanical energy. Principle: current-carrying coil in magnetic field experiences torque (rotates). DC motor: armature (coil), split-ring commutator (reverses current every half-turn), brushes, magnets. Commercial motor: many turns, electromagnet, laminated core.

chunks.title: Understanding Electric Motor
chunks.content:
**Electric Motor** is a key topic in Magnetic Effects of Electric Current for CBSE Class X Science.

**Electric motor:** A device that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy (rotation).

**Principle:** A current-carrying coil placed in a magnetic field experiences a torque and rotates (Fleming's left-hand rule).

**DC motor:** Most common type for low-power applications.

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Construction and Working of DC Motor
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Parts:** Armature (rectangular coil ABCD of insulated Cu wire), Strong magnet (N-S), Split-ring commutator (two halves, reverses current every ½ turn), Carbon brushes (fixed, contact with commutator), Battery/DC supply
• **Working:** Current enters through brush → commutator → coil → magnetic force (up on one side, down on other) → coil rotates. After ½ turn, commutator halves swap connections → current direction in coil reverses → continuous rotation
• **Split ring commutator:** KEY component for DC motor. Ensures torque is always in same direction

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Commercial Motors and Improvements
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Electromagnet instead of permanent magnet:** Stronger field → more torque
• **Many turns (armature coil):** More turns = greater force (F=nBIL)
• **Laminated soft iron core:** Concentrates magnetic field, reduces eddy current losses
• **Multiple poles:** Smoother rotation, more uniform torque
• **Uses:** Fans, pumps, electric vehicles, toys, elevators, washing machines, drills

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** Explain the role of split-ring commutator in a DC motor.

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** Coil rotates in magnetic field due to torque
**Step 2:** After half turn, the sides of coil that were up are now down
**Step 3:** Without commutator, current would create opposite torque → coil would stop
**Step 4:** Split-ring commutator reverses current direction in coil every half turn
**Step 5:** Result: torque always in same direction → continuous rotation

**Answer:** Commutator reverses current every ½ turn, ensuring continuous rotation in same direction.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What is the principle of an electric motor?

**Answer:** Current-carrying coil in magnetic field experiences torque → rotates (Fleming's LH rule).

---

**Q2.** Name the device that reverses current direction in a DC motor.

**Answer:** Split-ring commutator.

---

**Q3.** What happens if the split-ring commutator is replaced by slip rings?

**Answer:** Current does NOT reverse. Coil oscillates back and forth (wouldn't rotate continuously). Slip rings are for AC generators.

598d4371324c8ea7.summary: Electromagnetic induction (Faraday, 1831): changing magnetic field induces current in a conductor. Faraday's experiments with coil and magnet. Induced EMF depends on rate of change of flux, number of turns, magnetic field strength. Applications: generators, transformers, induction cooktops.

chunks.title: Understanding Electromagnetic Induction
chunks.content:
**Electromagnetic Induction** is a key topic in Magnetic Effects of Electric Current for CBSE Class X Science.

**Michael Faraday (1831):** Discovered that a changing magnetic field can induce an electric current in a conductor.

**Electromagnetic induction:** The process of generating electric current by changing the magnetic field. This is the reverse of the motor effect.

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Faraday's Experiments
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Experiment 1:** Push bar magnet into coil → galvanometer deflects (current induced). Stop moving → no deflection. Pull out → opposite deflection. Faster motion → more deflection
• **Experiment 2:** Primary coil connected to battery through switch. Secondary coil connected to galvanometer. Closing/opening switch → momentary deflection in secondary (changing current → changing magnetic field)
• **Key points:** Induced current exists ONLY while magnetic field is CHANGING. Direction depends on whether field increases/decreases. Rate of change of flux determines magnitude

chunks.title: Key Concepts of Fleming's Right-Hand Rule
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Rule:** Stretch right hand with thumb, forefinger, middle finger mutually perpendicular
• **Forefinger:** Direction of Magnetic Field (N→S)
• **Thumb:** Direction of Motion of conductor
• **Middle finger:** Direction of Induced Current
• **Factors affecting induced EMF:** Speed of motion (faster → more), Strength of magnetic field (stronger → more), Number of turns (more → higher)

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A magnet is moved towards a coil. What happens when the magnet is moved (a) faster (b) stopped inside (c) pulled away?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** (a) Faster → larger deflection (more rate of change of flux)
**Step 2:** (b) Stopped → no deflection (no change in flux)
**Step 3:** (c) Pulled away → deflection in opposite direction (flux decreasing)

**Answer:** (a) Large deflection. (b) No deflection. (c) Opposite deflection.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** Who discovered electromagnetic induction?

**Answer:** Michael Faraday (1831).

---

**Q2.** State Fleming's right-hand rule.

**Answer:** Thumb = Motion, Forefinger = Field (N→S), Middle finger = Induced Current.

---

**Q3.** How can the magnitude of induced current be increased?

**Answer:** Move magnet faster, use stronger magnet, increase coil turns, use larger coil area.

bcabfb804d0178d1.summary: Electric generator converts mechanical → electrical energy. AC generator uses slip rings (current changes direction). DC generator uses split-ring commutator (unidirectional). Principle: electromagnetic induction. National grid: step-up (transmission) and step-down (distribution) transformers.

chunks.title: Understanding Electric Generator
chunks.content:
**Electric Generator** is a key topic in Magnetic Effects of Electric Current for CBSE Class X Science.

**Electric generator:** Device that converts mechanical energy into electrical energy.

**Principle:** Electromagnetic induction (Faraday's law). When a coil rotates in a magnetic field, the magnetic flux through it changes → induced EMF.

**Types:** AC generator (alternating current) and DC generator (direct current).

chunks.title: Key Concepts of AC Generator
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **Construction:** Coil (armature) rotated in magnetic field. Slip rings (complete rings, one at each end) + carbon brushes. Magnet (N-S)
• **Working:** Coil rotates → magnetic flux through coil changes → induced current. After ½ turn, direction of induced current reverses. After full turn, one complete AC cycle. Slip rings provide continuous contact with external circuit
• **Graph:** Sinusoidal wave. Frequency = rotations per second × pairs of poles. India: 220 V, 50 Hz (50 cycles per second)
• **Advantages of AC:** Can be transformed (voltage stepped up/down), less power loss in transmission, simpler generators

chunks.title: Key Concepts of DC Generator and National Grid
chunks.content:
**Important points:**
• **DC generator:** Same as AC generator but has SPLIT-RING COMMUTATOR instead of slip rings. Produces current in one direction (but still pulsing)
• **Transformer:** Changes AC voltage. Step-up (increases V, decreases I) for long-distance transmission. Step-down (decreases V, increases I) for household use. Power conservation: P = VI (constant)

chunks.title: Solved Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** What is the difference between AC and DC generator?

**Solution:**
**Step 1:** AC generator uses SLIP RINGS (continuous rings). DC generator uses SPLIT-RING COMMUTATOR (split into two halves)
**Step 2:** AC: current direction reverses each half cycle. DC: current always in same direction (but pulsing)
**Step 3:** AC: sinusoidal waveform. DC: unidirectional but varying (can be smoothed)

**Answer:** AC: slip rings → alternating current. DC: split-ring → unidirectional current.

chunks.title: Practice Questions
chunks.content:
**Q1.** What is the principle of an electric generator?

**Answer:** Electromagnetic induction: rotating coil in magnetic field induces current.

---

**Q2.** Why is AC preferred for power transmission?

**Answer:** AC voltage can be stepped up (less I → less I²R loss in transmission lines). DC cannot be easily transformed.

---

**Q3.** Name the device used to increase AC voltage.

**Answer:** Step-up transformer (increases V, decreases I).
